methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series C → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.7x) to $40M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.rationale: Median across 3 same-sector + same-stage peers in Climate & Energy. Range: $700M - $3.0B.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Climate & Bio-Manufacturing

private.name: KoBold Metals
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series C), post-money $2.96B

private.name: Electric Hydrogen
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series C), post-money $1B

private.name: Amogy
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series C), post-money $700M

data.narrative:
Forge Nano is a Series C-stage climate and energy technology company that has raised $40M to date. The valuation analysis draws on a peer universe of 9 private companies and 1 public comparable (Lilium, ticker LNZA), with particular focus on three same-sector, same-stage private peers: KoBold Metals ($3.0B), Electric Hydrogen ($1.0B), and Amogy ($700M).

Two triangulation methods were applied. The stage-progression approach applies a typical Series C step-up multiplier of 1.7x to the $40M total raised proxy, yielding an implied valuation of $68M. The peer-median method uses the three identified climate and energy Series C comparables, which trade at a median valuation of $1.0B, with a range spanning $700M to $3.0B. This peer-derived multiple reflects current market pricing for frontier-tech capital intensity and development timelines in the sector.

The triangulated range spans $58M (low) to $1.1B (high), with a median of $534M. The wide dispersion reflects material uncertainty in technology commercialization risk and capital requirements typical of early-stage climate infrastructure. The stage-progression method anchors conservatively; the peer-median method incorporates recent market consensus for the sector. Resolution of this range will depend on evidence of technical validation, customer adoption trajectories, and capital efficiency relative to peer benchmarks.
